topics.title: Virtual DOM & Reconciliation (Fiber)
topics.summary: React's VDOM diff algorithm — Fiber makes reconciliation interruptible.
topics.explanation: React maintains an in-memory Virtual DOM (Fiber tree). On state changes: Render Phase (async, interruptible) — React calls your component, creates a new Fiber tree, diffs it with the old one using its heuristic O(n) algorithm. Key heuristics: (1) Elements of different types → unmount old, mount new. (2) Same type → update props. (3) Lists → use keys to match siblings. Commit Phase (synchronous) — applies minimal DOM mutations. React Fiber (v16+) splits work into units that can be paused/prioritized for Concurrent Features.
topics.analogy: The Virtual DOM is like editing a draft of a document before printing: React compares the new draft with the old one and only re-prints the lines that actually changed, instead of reprinting the whole page.
keyPoints: React diffs a new Fiber tree against the old one with an O(n) heuristic.
Render phase is async/interruptible; commit phase is synchronous.
Different element type → replace; same type → update props.
Lists need stable keys so React can match items across renders.
Fiber (v16+) splits work into pausable units → enables Concurrent features.
topics.imageCaption: React Fiber — splits reconciliation into interruptible work units, enabling Concurrent Mode

situations.question: After adding 200 items to a list with index keys, reordering them causes every item to re-render and lose state. How do you fix it?
situations.answer: Using index as key means React can't track item identity across reorders. When you move item[0] to position [5], React sees position [5] has different content and re-creates it — losing focus, animation state, or input values. Fix: use a stable unique ID as key (item.id, item.slug, crypto.randomUUID()). If items truly have no ID, generate one during data creation, not at render time. For virtualized lists (react-window), stable keys are especially critical — wrong keys cause the entire visible window to re-render.

topics.title: JSX & React Elements
topics.summary: JSX compiles to React.createElement() — understand what it produces.
topics.explanation: JSX is syntactic sugar. Babel compiles '<Button color="blue">Click</Button>' to 'React.createElement(Button, { color: "blue" }, "Click")'. This returns a plain JS object (React element): { type: Button, props: { color: 'blue', children: 'Click' }, key: null, ref: null }. React elements are immutable descriptions — they describe what to render, not the rendered output. React 17+ introduced the automatic JSX transform (no need to import React for JSX).
topics.analogy: JSX is a shorthand the way emoji shortcodes are: ':smile:' becomes 😄. '<Button/>' is just sugar that Babel turns into a plain React.createElement(...) object describing what to render.
keyPoints: JSX compiles to React.createElement(type, props, children).
An element is a plain, immutable object — a description, not the rendered DOM.
{ type, props, key, ref } is its shape.
React 17+ auto JSX transform — no need to import React for JSX.
Elements describe WHAT to render; React decides HOW.

situations.question: A junior dev is confused why returning 'null' from a component doesn't crash but returning 'undefined' does. Explain.
situations.answer: React explicitly handles 'null', 'false', and undefined-ish returns: returning null is valid (renders nothing). Returning undefined was historically an error — React throws because undefined usually signals a bug (forgot to return). React 18 relaxed this for server components but client components still warn. Returning false or null is the idiomatic way to conditionally render nothing: '{isVisible && <Modal />}' — when isVisible is false, React renders nothing. For empty renders, prefer 'return null' over 'return undefined'.

topics.title: Error Boundaries
topics.summary: Class components catching render-time errors — show fallback UI per widget.
topics.explanation: Error Boundaries catch JavaScript errors in their child tree during rendering, lifecycle methods, and constructors. They cannot catch: event handler errors (use try/catch), async errors, SSR errors, or their own errors. Use react-error-boundary library for a functional approach with a resetKeys prop to auto-recover. Pattern: wrap every independent section (widget, route, feature) in its own boundary for granular recovery. React 19 adds support for async error boundaries.
topics.analogy: An Error Boundary is a circuit breaker per room: if one appliance shorts out, only that room loses power and shows a fallback, instead of the whole house going dark.
keyPoints: Catch render/lifecycle/constructor errors in their child tree.
Do NOT catch event-handler, async, or SSR errors — handle those manually.
Wrap each independent widget/route in its own boundary for isolation.
react-error-boundary adds a clean functional API with resetKeys.
Always keep a top-level boundary as a last resort.

situations.question: One widget in your dashboard crashes and takes the entire page down. How do you architect error resilience?
situations.answer: Wrap each independent section in its own ErrorBoundary. Use react-error-boundary with: 1) FallbackComponent showing retry button. 2) onError callback to log to Sentry with component stack. 3) resetKeys to auto-recover when the relevant data changes. 4) For async data errors (fetch failures), handle in the component — they don't propagate to ErrorBoundary. 5) Add a global boundary at App root as last resort. Pattern: App > GlobalBoundary > Routes > PageBoundary > WidgetBoundary > Widget.

topics.title: useState
topics.summary: Local state. Batched async updates. Functional updates for derived state.
topics.explanation: useState returns [value, setter]. Updates are asynchronous and batched in React 18+. Key: the setter can take a value OR a function — always use the functional form when new state depends on previous state to avoid stale closures. Lazy initialization: pass a function to useState to run initialization only once. React compares with Object.is() — same value reference bails out of re-render.
topics.analogy: useState is a labeled box React keeps for your component between renders: you read what's in it and hand React a new value, and React re-renders to show it.
keyPoints: Returns [value, setter]; updates are async and batched (React 18+).
Use the functional form setX(prev => …) when new state depends on old.
Pass a function for lazy init — it runs only once on mount.
React bails out of re-render if the new value is Object.is-equal.
For objects/arrays you must spread manually — state is immutable.

topics.title: useEffect
topics.summary: Side effects after paint. Runs after DOM update. Cleanup on unmount/re-run.
topics.explanation: Runs after every render by default. Dependency array: [] = mount only, [deps] = when deps change. Cleanup function runs before the next effect AND on unmount — critical for unsubscribing, canceling fetches, clearing timers. React 18 Strict Mode double-invokes effects in development to expose missing cleanups. Rule: if you read a value inside useEffect, it must be in the deps array (ESLint react-hooks/exhaustive-deps enforces this).
topics.analogy: useEffect is a chore you do AFTER the room is painted (rendered): sync with the outside world (fetch, subscribe, timers), and the cleanup function is putting your tools away before you redo the chore or leave.
keyPoints: Runs after paint; deps array controls when it re-runs ([] = mount only).
Cleanup runs before the next effect AND on unmount.
Every value you read inside must be in the deps (exhaustive-deps).
Strict Mode double-invokes in dev to expose missing cleanup.
Use for side effects, not for deriving values during render.

situations.question: Your useEffect fires twice in development but once in production. Why? Is this a bug?
situations.answer: React 18 Strict Mode intentionally double-fires effects in development (mount → cleanup → mount) to expose missing cleanup functions. This is NOT a bug — it's React verifying your cleanup works correctly. If your effect has visible side effects (doubled API calls, doubled analytics events), the fix is proper cleanup: add AbortController to cancel the first fetch, store subscription references to remove them. In production, effects run only once per mount. Never add a 'ran once' ref to bypass this — you're hiding real bugs.

topics.title: useContext
topics.summary: Consume context without prop drilling. Re-renders ALL consumers on value change.
topics.explanation: useContext subscribes a component to a context. The component re-renders EVERY time the context value changes — even if the component only uses a tiny part of it. This is why context splitting matters: separate AuthContext, ThemeContext, CartContext so unrelated consumers don't re-render together. Combine with useMemo for the context value to prevent spurious re-renders when the Provider re-renders.
topics.analogy: Context is a building-wide intercom: instead of passing a message person-to-person (prop drilling), you broadcast it and any room can tune in — but everyone tuned in re-hears it whenever it changes.
keyPoints: Reads a context value without prop drilling.
EVERY consumer re-renders when the value changes — even partial users.
Split contexts by concern (Auth/Theme/Cart) to limit re-renders.
Memoize the Provider value to avoid spurious re-renders.
For high-frequency updates, prefer Zustand/Jotai (atomic subscriptions).

situations.question: Changing a checkbox in your app re-renders 50 unrelated components. Context is involved. How do you diagnose and fix?
situations.answer: When a Context Provider re-renders with a new value reference, ALL consumers re-render. Fixes: 1) Memoize the context value: 'useMemo(() => ({ user, setUser }), [user])' — only changes when user changes. 2) Split context — if checkbox state is in the same context as user, separate them. 3) Use Zustand or Jotai — components subscribe to only the atoms they use, not the whole store. 4) Diagnose: React DevTools > Profiler > 'Highlight updates' shows which components re-render and why.

topics.title: useReducer
topics.summary: State machine pattern. Complex state logic. dispatch(action) → new state.
topics.explanation: Alternative to useState for complex state logic. Pure reducer function (state, action) => newState. Dispatch actions with descriptive types — makes state transitions explicit and testable. useReducer + useContext = lightweight global state management (like Redux without the boilerplate). useReducer is preferred when: next state depends on multiple sub-values, you want a predictable audit trail of changes, or state logic is complex enough to test separately.
topics.analogy: useReducer is a vending machine for state: you press a labeled button (dispatch an action) and a pure function decides exactly what comes out (the next state) — predictable and testable.
keyPoints: (state, action) => newState — a pure, testable reducer.
Better than useState when state is complex or transitions are many.
Actions make state changes explicit and auditable.
useReducer + Context ≈ lightweight Redux without the library.
dispatch identity is stable — safe to omit from deps.

topics.title: useMemo
topics.summary: Cache expensive computation result. Recompute only when deps change.
topics.explanation: useMemo caches the return VALUE of a function between renders. Recomputes when deps change. Use for: expensive computations (filter/sort large lists), stable object/array references passed as props or context values, avoiding redundant calculations. Don't use for: trivial computations, preventing renders (use React.memo for that). Overhead: each render compares deps with Object.is() — for cheap computations the comparison cost > recomputation cost.
topics.analogy: useMemo is keeping the result of a slow calculation on a sticky note: as long as the inputs haven't changed, hand back the note instead of recalculating.
keyPoints: Caches a computed VALUE between renders; recomputes when deps change.
Use for expensive computations and stable object/array references.
Don't use for trivial work — the deps comparison can cost more.
Pair with React.memo when passing memoized props down.
Not a correctness tool — React may discard the cache.

topics.title: useCallback
topics.summary: Cache function reference. Prevents memo'd children from re-rendering.
topics.explanation: useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). Returns the same function reference unless deps change. Critical when: passing callbacks to React.memo'd child components, using functions as useEffect dependencies, or passing handlers to deeply nested trees. Without useCallback, every parent render creates a new function reference, breaking React.memo's shallow prop comparison.
topics.analogy: useCallback is useMemo for functions: it hands back the SAME function instance between renders, so memoized children don't think they got a new prop and re-render.
keyPoints: useCallback(fn, deps) ≡ useMemo(() => fn, deps).
Returns a stable function reference unless deps change.
Only helps when the child is React.memo'd (shallow prop compare).
Also useful when a function is a useEffect dependency.
Without it, every render makes a new function, breaking memoization.

topics.title: useRef
topics.summary: Mutable .current box — DOM access, timer IDs, previous values. No re-render.
topics.explanation: useRef returns a mutable object { current: initialValue } that persists across renders WITHOUT triggering re-renders. Two primary uses: 1) DOM access — pass as ref prop to get the actual DOM element. 2) Mutable instance variable — store timer IDs, previous prop values, AbortControllers, or the 'latest' version of a prop/state (useLatesRef pattern). Mutating .current is synchronous and immediate, unlike setState.
topics.analogy: useRef is a pocket your component carries across renders: you can stash things in it (a DOM node, a timer id, the latest value) and changing what's inside doesn't trigger a re-render.
keyPoints: Returns a mutable { current } that persists across renders.
Changing .current does NOT cause a re-render.
Use for DOM access and mutable instance values (timers, controllers).
The 'latest ref' pattern keeps a fresh value inside stable callbacks.
Mutations are synchronous, unlike setState.

topics.title: useLayoutEffect
topics.summary: Like useEffect but fires synchronously after DOM mutations, before paint.
topics.explanation: useLayoutEffect runs AFTER the DOM is updated but BEFORE the browser paints. Blocks the paint until it completes. Use when: you need to read DOM measurements (element dimensions, scroll position) and update state/DOM before the user sees anything — prevents visual flicker. For most cases, useEffect is correct and preferable (non-blocking). useLayoutEffect on the server (SSR) causes a warning — guard with 'typeof window !== undefined'.
topics.analogy: useLayoutEffect is measuring and adjusting furniture BEFORE the guests see the room: it runs after the DOM updates but before paint, so you can fix positions without any visible flicker.
keyPoints: Runs synchronously after DOM mutation, before the browser paints.
Blocks paint — use only when you must measure/adjust to avoid flicker.
Default to useEffect (non-blocking) for almost everything.
Common use: tooltip/positioning that reads getBoundingClientRect.
Warns during SSR — guard with an isomorphic effect helper.

topics.title: useImperativeHandle
topics.summary: Customize what's exposed via ref to parent components. Use with forwardRef.
topics.explanation: useImperativeHandle customizes the ref object exposed to the parent when using forwardRef (or the new ref-as-prop in React 19). Instead of exposing the entire DOM node, you expose only specific methods. This keeps the component's internal implementation hidden while providing a clean imperative API. Common use: custom input components with focus(), reset(), select() methods; video players with play(), pause(); modals with open(), close().
topics.analogy: useImperativeHandle is a remote control with only the buttons you choose: instead of handing the parent your whole DOM node, you expose a tidy API like focus(), clear(), play().
keyPoints: Customizes what a ref exposes to the parent.
Used with forwardRef (or ref-as-prop in React 19).
Expose specific methods, hiding internal implementation.
Great for inputs (focus/clear), players (play/pause), modals (open/close).
Keep deps empty/stable so the handle reference is consistent.

topics.title: useId
topics.summary: Generate stable unique IDs for accessibility — safe for SSR hydration.
topics.explanation: useId generates a unique, stable ID that is consistent between server and client renders — solving the SSR hydration mismatch problem that random IDs (Math.random(), UUID) cause. The ID is stable across re-renders of the component. Use for: form label/input pairing (htmlFor + id), aria-labelledby, aria-describedby. Don't use as list keys — it's not designed for that.
topics.analogy: useId is a name-tag machine that prints the SAME tag on both the server and the client, so your accessibility wiring (label↔input) doesn't break during hydration the way random IDs would.
keyPoints: Generates a unique ID stable across SSR and client hydration.
Solves hydration mismatches caused by Math.random()/UUID.
Use for label/input pairing, aria-labelledby, aria-describedby.
Stable across re-renders of the same component.
Don't use it as a list key.

topics.title: useTransition
topics.summary: Mark updates as non-urgent. Keeps UI responsive during heavy re-renders.
topics.explanation: useTransition returns [isPending, startTransition]. State updates inside startTransition are marked as non-urgent — React can interrupt them to process urgent updates (typing, clicking). isPending is true while the transition is in progress — use to show a loading indicator. The non-urgent update may be deferred by multiple frames. Critical for: search filtering large lists, tab switching, page transitions. Combined with Suspense for data fetching.
topics.analogy: useTransition is letting someone with one urgent item skip ahead of your big cart: typing stays instant (urgent) while the heavy re-render waits its turn and can be interrupted.
keyPoints: Returns [isPending, startTransition].
Updates inside startTransition are non-urgent and interruptible.
isPending lets you show a subtle loading state.
Great for filtering big lists, tab switches, page transitions.
Wraps the UPDATER (vs useDeferredValue which wraps the value).

situations.question: Your search input stutters on every keystroke because it filters 10,000 items. How do you fix it?
situations.answer: Wrap the filter state update in startTransition. The input value (query) updates instantly (urgent), while the filter computation (results) is marked non-urgent and can be interrupted. React will prioritize keeping the input responsive. Additionally: memoize the filter with useMemo so it only re-runs when deferredQuery changes. Add useDeferredValue as an alternative approach. For 10k items: also consider Web Worker for the filter computation so it runs off the main thread entirely.

topics.title: useDeferredValue
topics.summary: Defer rendering an expensive computation — shows stale content during update.
topics.explanation: useDeferredValue accepts a value and returns a deferred version. During an urgent update, the deferred value stays stale while the urgent part renders. Once React has time, it re-renders with the latest deferred value. Different from useTransition: useTransition wraps the UPDATER, useDeferredValue wraps the VALUE. Use when you don't control the state update source (e.g., the value comes from props or an external store).
topics.analogy: useDeferredValue is showing yesterday's newspaper until today's is printed: the expensive view keeps the slightly-stale value while urgent updates (typing) stay snappy, then catches up.
keyPoints: Returns a deferred copy of a value that lags during urgent updates.
Keeps the UI responsive while expensive renders catch up.
Wraps the VALUE (vs useTransition which wraps the updater).
Use when you don't control where the value comes from (props/store).
Compare value !== deferredValue to show a 'stale' indicator.

topics.title: useSyncExternalStore
topics.summary: Subscribe to external stores (Redux, Zustand, browser APIs) — tearing-safe.
topics.explanation: The correct way to subscribe to external data stores in React 18 Concurrent Mode. Prevents 'tearing' — where different components see different versions of the same external state during a concurrent render. Every state management library (Redux, Zustand, Jotai) uses this internally. You can use it directly to subscribe to browser APIs (window.online, localStorage, document.visibilityState).
topics.analogy: useSyncExternalStore is a single official scoreboard everyone reads: it guarantees every component sees the same external value at once, so no two parts of the UI 'tear' apart with mismatched data.
keyPoints: The correct way to subscribe to external stores in Concurrent React.
Prevents 'tearing' (components seeing different versions mid-render).
Takes subscribe, getSnapshot, and getServerSnapshot.
Redux/Zustand/Jotai use it internally.
Use it directly for browser APIs (online status, visibility).

topics.title: useDebugValue
topics.summary: Label custom hooks in React DevTools for easier debugging.
topics.explanation: useDebugValue displays a label in React DevTools next to a custom hook. Only visible in DevTools — no effect on production. Accept a formatter function as second argument for expensive formatting that only runs when DevTools is open. Use in any custom hook to make it easily identifiable in DevTools.
topics.analogy: useDebugValue is a name label you stick on a custom hook so it's recognizable in the React DevTools toolbox — purely for your debugging convenience.
keyPoints: Shows a label next to a custom hook in React DevTools.
DevTools-only — zero effect in production.
Pass a formatter as the 2nd arg to defer expensive formatting.
Use inside reusable custom hooks for clarity.
Never affects rendering or behavior.

topics.title: Actions (React 19)
topics.summary: Async functions as form actions — automatic pending, error, and optimistic state.
topics.explanation: React 19 introduces Actions — async functions that handle data mutations. When you pass an async function to a form's action prop or use startTransition, React automatically manages: pending state, error handling, and optimistic updates. This replaces the common useState(isLoading) + try/catch pattern. Works with both client-side and server-side mutations (Server Actions in Next.js).
topics.analogy: Actions are an autopilot for form submissions: hand React an async function and it automatically flies the pending spinner, error handling, and optimistic updates for you.
keyPoints: Async functions wired to a form's action prop or startTransition.
React auto-manages pending, error, and optimistic state.
Replaces the manual isLoading + try/catch + setError pattern.
Works with client and server (Next.js Server Actions) mutations.
Pairs with useActionState and useOptimistic.

situations.question: You have a form with complex validation, optimistic updates, and error rollback. Previously this required 5 useStates. How does React 19 simplify this?
situations.answer: React 19's useActionState + useOptimistic reduces the boilerplate dramatically: 1) useActionState handles: pending state (isPending), error state, and action result in one hook. 2) useOptimistic handles: showing immediate feedback before server response, auto-rolling back if the action fails. 3) No manual setIsLoading, setError, setSuccessMessage needed. The form action prop handles the entire lifecycle. For server forms with Next.js, Server Actions integrate seamlessly — the form posts to the server action function directly.

topics.title: useActionState (React 19)
topics.summary: Replaces useState + isPending + error pattern for form mutations.
topics.explanation: useActionState(action, initialState, permalink?) returns [state, formAction, isPending]. The action receives previous state + FormData. React automatically handles pending state — isPending is true while the async action runs. Errors in the action are caught and returned as state. Works with both client actions and server actions (Next.js). Replaces the common 'isLoading + error + success' triple useState pattern.
topics.analogy: useActionState bundles the three things every form needs — is it submitting, did it error, what's the result — into one tidy package, instead of juggling three separate useState calls.
keyPoints: Returns [state, formAction, isPending].
The action receives (prevState, formData).
isPending is managed automatically while the action runs.
Errors returned from the action become state.
Works with both client and server actions.

topics.title: useOptimistic (React 19)
topics.summary: Show immediate UI feedback before server response. Auto-reverts on failure.
topics.explanation: useOptimistic(state, updateFn) returns [optimisticState, addOptimistic]. When you call addOptimistic(optimisticValue), it immediately applies the updateFn to create an optimistic (predicted) state shown to the user. When the async action completes: if successful, the real state takes over. If failed, the optimistic state is automatically discarded and the original state is shown. This creates the 'instant feedback, eventual consistency' UX pattern used by Twitter/X likes, Instagram reactions.
topics.analogy: useOptimistic is showing a 'sent' message the instant you hit send (like chat apps): the UI updates immediately, and if the server later fails, React quietly rolls it back.
keyPoints: Returns [optimisticState, addOptimistic].
Instantly shows a predicted state before the server responds.
Auto-reverts to real state on success or failure.
Creates the 'instant feedback, eventual consistency' UX.
Pairs with Actions / form submissions.

topics.title: use() Hook (React 19)
topics.summary: Unwrap Promises and Context inside conditions/loops — breaks Rules of Hooks.
topics.explanation: The 'use' hook is unique — it can be called inside conditionals, loops, and early returns (unlike other hooks). It can: 1) Unwrap a Promise — suspends the component until the promise resolves (pairs with Suspense). 2) Read context like useContext but conditionally. When use(promise) suspends, the nearest Suspense boundary shows its fallback. On rejection, the nearest ErrorBoundary catches it.
topics.analogy: use() is the rule-breaker hook: unlike the others it can sit inside ifs and loops. Give it a Promise and the component politely 'pauses' (suspends) until the data arrives.
keyPoints: Can be called conditionally / in loops (unlike other hooks).
use(promise) suspends until it resolves (pairs with Suspense).
use(Context) reads context, even after an early return.
Rejected promises bubble to the nearest Error Boundary.
Enables clean data-reading in modern React.

topics.title: useFormStatus (React 19)
topics.summary: Access parent form's pending state from child components — no prop drilling.
topics.explanation: useFormStatus() returns { pending, data, method, action } — reads the status of the nearest parent form. The key insight: you don't need to pass 'isLoading' down as a prop — any child component inside the form can call useFormStatus() to know if the form is submitting. Particularly useful for Submit button components that need to know when to disable.
topics.analogy: useFormStatus is a child checking the parent form's pulse directly: a submit button deep inside the form can ask 'are we submitting?' without anyone passing it a prop.
keyPoints: Returns { pending, data, method, action } of the nearest form.
Lets child components read submit state without prop drilling.
Perfect for reusable Submit buttons that self-disable.
Imported from react-dom (not react).
Works automatically inside any <form action={…}>.

topics.title: ref as a Prop (React 19)
topics.summary: No more forwardRef — pass ref like any other prop in React 19.
topics.explanation: In React 19, 'ref' is treated as a regular prop for function components. You no longer need forwardRef() to pass refs to child components. This simplifies component APIs and removes the extra wrapper. The old forwardRef API still works but is deprecated. This change also enables TypeScript to properly type ref props without the forwardRef generic gymnastics.
topics.analogy: In React 19, ref grew up: it's now just a normal prop you can pass straight to a function component — no more wrapping everything in forwardRef.
keyPoints: Function components can accept ref as a regular prop.
forwardRef is no longer required (and is now deprecated).
Simplifies component APIs and TypeScript typing.
Usage at the call site is unchanged.
Old forwardRef code still works during migration.

topics.title: React Server Components (RSC)
topics.summary: Render on server only — async by default, zero client JS, direct DB access.
topics.explanation: RSC are components that render only on the server. Zero client-side JS footprint — their code never ships to the browser. They can: be async functions (await DB/API calls at the top level), import server-only packages (credentials stay safe), access file system. They CANNOT: use state, effects, browser APIs, event handlers. In Next.js App Router, all components are Server Components by default. Mark client components with 'use client'. Pass data from RSC to Client Components via props.
topics.analogy: Server Components are a chef who cooks in the kitchen and sends out finished plates: they run only on the server (with direct DB access), and the browser receives ready HTML with zero JS shipped for them.
keyPoints: Render only on the server — their code never ships to the browser.
Can be async and access DB/filesystem/secrets directly.
Cannot use state, effects, browser APIs, or event handlers.
Default in Next.js App Router; mark interactivity with 'use client'.
Pass data to Client Components via props.

situations.question: Your Next.js product page loads in 3 seconds because it fetches data client-side. How do you use RSC to improve this?
situations.answer: Convert the page component to async RSC. Instead of useEffect + fetch, write 'const product = await db.product.findById(id)' directly in the component. React renders the page on the server with data embedded — HTML arrives at the browser already populated, no client-side fetch needed, LCP improves dramatically. Keep interactive parts (add to cart, wishlist) as 'use client' components. For dynamic data: use Next.js fetch() cache: use React.cache() to deduplicate server-side fetches across components in the same render.

sections.title: State Management — Redux vs Context vs Zustand

topics.title: When to Use What — Decision Framework
topics.summary: Local state → useState. Shared simple state → Context. Complex/global → Zustand/Redux.
topics.explanation: Choose based on scope, complexity, and performance: Local state (single component) → useState or useReducer. Shared state (parent + 2-3 children, low-frequency updates) → Context + useReducer. Global state (many components, frequent updates, complex logic) → Zustand or Redux Toolkit. Server state (API data, caching, sync) → TanStack Query or SWR — NEVER store server state in Redux. Form state → React Hook Form. URL state (filters, pagination) → URL search params.
topics.analogy: Choosing state is like choosing storage: a backpack for what's on you (local useState), a shared shelf for the household (Context), a warehouse for everyone (Zustand/Redux), and a fridge for perishable server data (TanStack Query).
keyPoints: Local → useState/useReducer; shared & simple → Context.
Global, frequent, complex → Zustand or Redux Toolkit.
Server data → TanStack Query/SWR (never store it in Redux).
Form state → React Hook Form; URL state → search params.
Separate server state from UI state, and frequent from rare updates.

situations.question: In an interview, you're asked: 'We have 50 components that need access to the current user, theme, and cart. How do you manage state?' What do you say?
situations.answer: Split by domain and update frequency: 1) Current user (auth) → Context + useReducer: rarely changes, all components need it. Memoize the value. 2) Theme → Context: very rarely changes, no performance concern. 3) Cart state (frequent add/remove) → Zustand: frequently updated, many components read it. Context would cause re-renders on every add-to-cart. Zustand with atomic selectors means only cart-count components re-render when count changes. 4) Cart data from server → TanStack Query: server state, not UI state. Design principle: separate server state from UI state, separate frequently-changing from rarely-changing.

topics.title: Redux Toolkit (RTK)
topics.summary: Slices, createAsyncThunk, RTK Query — the modern way to use Redux.
topics.explanation: Redux Toolkit (RTK) is the official, recommended way to write Redux. Eliminates boilerplate: createSlice auto-generates action creators + reducers + action types. Uses Immer internally — write 'mutating' code that's actually immutable. createAsyncThunk handles async actions with pending/fulfilled/rejected states. RTK Query is a powerful data fetching and caching solution integrated with Redux. Use Redux/RTK when: large team (strict conventions help), complex cross-feature interactions, need time-travel debugging, or the app already uses Redux.
topics.analogy: Redux Toolkit is Redux with the paperwork pre-filled: createSlice writes the actions and reducers for you, and Immer lets you 'mutate' state that's actually updated immutably under the hood.
keyPoints: Official, boilerplate-free way to write Redux.
createSlice auto-generates actions + reducers + types.
Immer lets you write mutating-looking, truly-immutable updates.
createAsyncThunk handles pending/fulfilled/rejected.
RTK Query handles data fetching + caching; best for large/structured apps.

topics.title: Zustand
topics.summary: Minimal, hook-based store. Atomic selectors. No Provider needed.
topics.explanation: Zustand is a small (~1KB) state management library. No Provider wrapper needed — store is external. Components subscribe via selector hooks — only re-renders when the selected slice changes. Supports: middleware (persist, devtools, immer), async actions, derived state. Perfect for small-to-medium apps or when Redux is overkill. Can be combined with TanStack Query for server state.
topics.analogy: Zustand is a shared whiteboard anyone can read or write without a Provider wrapper: components subscribe to just the lines they care about, so only they re-render when those change.
keyPoints: Tiny (~1KB) store with no Provider needed.
Selector hooks re-render only on the slice you select.
Middleware: persist, devtools, immer.
Great for small-to-medium global UI state.
Combine with TanStack Query for server state.

topics.title: TanStack Query (React Query)
topics.summary: Server state: caching, dedup, background refresh, pagination — replaces useEffect + fetch.
topics.explanation: TanStack Query manages server state: async data fetching, caching, deduplication, background refetching, and mutation handling. Key concepts: queryKey (cache identity), queryFn (fetch function), staleTime (how long cached data is fresh), gcTime (how long unused data stays in cache). Mutations with useMutation + onSuccess cache invalidation. Optimistic updates built-in. Replaces: useEffect data fetching, manual loading/error states, manual cache management. Works perfectly alongside Zustand (UI state) and Redux (app state).
topics.analogy: TanStack Query is a smart fridge for server data: it remembers what you fetched, knows when it's stale, restocks in the background, and avoids buying the same thing twice (dedup).
keyPoints: Manages server state: caching, dedup, background refetch, mutations.
Key concepts: queryKey, queryFn, staleTime, gcTime.
Replaces useEffect fetching and manual loading/error state.
Built-in optimistic updates and cache invalidation.
Pairs with Zustand (UI state) — they solve different problems.

topics.title: Code Splitting & Lazy Loading
topics.summary: Split JS bundle by route/feature. React.lazy + Suspense for component-level splitting.
topics.explanation: A large monolithic JS bundle delays Time to Interactive (TTI). Code splitting breaks it into smaller chunks — each route or feature loads only what it needs. React.lazy() + Suspense handles component-level splitting. Webpack/Vite split based on dynamic import() calls. Three strategies: Route-based (most impactful), Component-based (modals, heavy components), Library-based (split heavy dependencies like charts, maps).
topics.analogy: Code splitting is packing for a trip one bag per activity instead of one giant suitcase: users only carry (download) the code for the screen they're on, loading the rest on demand.
keyPoints: Breaks one big bundle into chunks loaded on demand.
React.lazy + Suspense splits at the component level.
Route-based splitting is the highest-impact strategy.
Also split heavy libraries (charts, editors) into their own chunk.
Prefetch the next likely chunk on hover to hide load latency.

situations.question: Your React app has a 3MB JS bundle. Users on 3G connections take 8 seconds to see anything. What's your systematic optimization plan?
situations.answer: 1) Analyze: 'npx source-map-explorer build/static/js/*.js' or Bundle Analyzer — find largest chunks. 2) Route splitting: every route = separate lazy chunk. 3) Remove heavy libraries: moment.js (2MB) → day.js (2KB). Import lodash as 'import debounce from lodash/debounce' not 'import _ from lodash'. 4) Dynamic import for heavy features: chart library only loads when user visits analytics. 5) Tree shaking: ensure all imports are ES modules. 6) Preload critical routes, prefetch others on hover. 7) Compress with Brotli (3x smaller than gzip). 8) Serve from CDN. Target: < 150KB initial bundle, rest lazy.

topics.title: React Rendering Performance
topics.summary: Memo, useCallback, virtualization — measure first with React Profiler.
topics.explanation: Over-rendering is the most common React performance issue. Toolbox: React.memo (skip child re-render on unchanged props), useCallback (stable function references), useMemo (stable computed values/objects), virtualization (react-window — only render visible rows), key stability (prevent unnecessary unmount/remount). Critical: MEASURE before optimizing — React DevTools Profiler shows exactly which components render and why.
topics.analogy: Fixing React perf is like fixing a noisy office: first find WHO is talking too much (Profiler), then quiet unnecessary re-renders (memo) and only show the rows on screen (virtualization).
keyPoints: Over-rendering is the most common React perf problem.
Tools: React.memo, useCallback, useMemo, key stability.
Virtualize long lists (react-window) — render only visible rows.
ALWAYS measure with the Profiler before optimizing.
Memoization only helps if props are actually stable.

topics.title: Core Web Vitals Optimization
topics.summary: LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms — each requires specific techniques.
topics.explanation: Core Web Vitals directly impact SEO (Google ranking factor) and user experience. LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): optimize the hero image — preload, WebP, fetchpriority='high', CDN. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): always set width/height on images, reserve space for dynamic content, use font-display: swap. INP (Interaction to Next Paint): break up long tasks with scheduler.yield(), move non-UI work to Web Workers, use startTransition for heavy re-renders. TTFB: server caching, edge computing, ISR.
topics.analogy: Core Web Vitals are a health check for your page: LCP = how fast the main content appears, CLS = how much the page jumps around, INP = how snappy it feels when tapped.
keyPoints: LCP <2.5s: preload hero image, WebP, fetchpriority, CDN.
CLS <0.1: set image width/height, reserve space, font-display: swap.
INP <200ms: break long tasks (scheduler.yield), workers, startTransition.
They're a Google SEO ranking factor.
Measure real users with the web-vitals library.

topics.title: XSS — Cross-Site Scripting
topics.summary: Stored, Reflected, DOM-based. React escapes by default. Sanitize dangerouslySetInnerHTML.
topics.explanation: XSS allows attackers to inject malicious scripts. Three types: Stored XSS (injected into DB, served to all visitors), Reflected XSS (via URL params), DOM-based XSS (client-side DOM manipulation without proper escaping). React escapes all JSX expressions by default. dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses this — always sanitize with DOMPurify before using. Content Security Policy (CSP) is the defense-in-depth — prevents execution of injected scripts even if XSS occurs.
topics.analogy: XSS is a forged note that the page reads aloud as code. React's default is to treat everything as plain text; dangerouslySetInnerHTML removes that safety net, so you must sanitize first.
keyPoints: Three types: Stored, Reflected, DOM-based.
React auto-escapes {expressions} by default.
dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses escaping — sanitize with DOMPurify.
Sanitize on both save and render (defense in depth).
Add a Content-Security-Policy as a last line of defense.

situations.question: A security audit found XSS in your rich text editor that uses dangerouslySetInnerHTML. You can't remove the feature. How do you fix it?
situations.answer: 1) Install DOMPurify. 2) Sanitize on BOTH save (server-side — before storing in DB) and render (client-side — before showing). Defense in depth. 3) Allow only safe tags: DOMPurify.sanitize(html, { ALLOWED_TAGS: ['p','b','i','ul','li','a'], ALLOWED_ATTR: ['href'], FORCE_BODY: true }). 4) For href: validate URLs — reject 'javascript:' scheme. 5) Add CSP header: 'script-src self' — blocks inline scripts even if XSS occurs. 6) Switch to a secure rich text editor: TipTap or Quill have XSS prevention built in. 7) Add automated security tests: unit test your sanitization with known XSS payloads.

topics.title: CSRF — Cross-Site Request Forgery
topics.summary: Attacker makes authenticated requests on user's behalf. CSRF tokens + SameSite cookies.
topics.explanation: CSRF tricks a user's browser into making authenticated requests to your server from a malicious site. The browser automatically sends cookies, so the request appears legitimate. Prevention: 1) SameSite=Strict/Lax cookies — browser only sends cookies on same-origin requests. 2) CSRF tokens — random token in form/header that server validates. 3) Verify Origin/Referer headers. 4) Use custom request headers (X-Requested-With) — cross-origin JS can't set custom headers without CORS. Modern apps using JWT in Authorization header (not cookies) are immune to CSRF.
topics.analogy: CSRF is a stranger mailing a withdrawal slip using your pre-signed signature (your cookie): the bank sees your signature and complies. Defenses prove the request really came from your app.
keyPoints: Tricks the browser into sending authenticated requests cross-site.
Cookies are sent automatically, making forged requests look legit.
Defenses: SameSite=Strict/Lax cookies + CSRF tokens.
Custom headers (X-Requested-With) can't be set cross-origin without CORS.
Tokens in the Authorization header (not cookies) are immune to CSRF.

topics.title: Content Security Policy (CSP)
topics.summary: HTTP header that whitelists allowed content sources — last line of defense vs XSS.
topics.explanation: CSP is an HTTP response header that tells the browser which content sources are trusted. Even if XSS occurs, CSP prevents the injected script from executing. Directives: script-src (JS sources), style-src (CSS), img-src, connect-src (fetch/XHR), font-src. 'nonce' approach: server generates random nonce per request, adds to trusted scripts — only scripts with matching nonce execute. Report-only mode: 'Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only' logs violations without blocking — use to test before enforcing.
topics.analogy: CSP is a guest list for your page's bouncer: even if an attacker sneaks a script in, the browser checks the list and refuses to run anything not from an approved source.
keyPoints: An HTTP header whitelisting trusted content sources.
Blocks injected scripts from executing even if XSS occurs.
Directives: script-src, style-src, img-src, connect-src, frame-ancestors.
Nonce/hash approach allows only specifically-trusted inline scripts.
Use Report-Only mode to test before enforcing.

topics.title: Authentication & Authorization Security
topics.summary: JWT storage, token refresh, HTTPS-only cookies, RBAC in frontend.
topics.explanation: JWT Storage: localStorage is accessible to JS (XSS vulnerable). httpOnly cookies are inaccessible to JS (XSS safe but CSRF risk — mitigate with SameSite). Best practice for SPAs: store access token in memory (short-lived, 15min), refresh token in httpOnly SameSite=Strict cookie. Frontend Authorization: never trust client-side role checks for security — they're only for UX. Always verify on the server. Route guards in React (ProtectedRoute) only prevent navigation, not API access.
topics.analogy: Token storage is choosing where to keep your house key: in your hand (memory — safest, lost on refresh) vs a tamper-proof lockbox the browser holds (httpOnly cookie). Either way, the real lock is on the server.
keyPoints: localStorage is XSS-readable; httpOnly cookies aren't (but need CSRF defense).
SPA best practice: access token in memory + refresh token in httpOnly cookie.
Keep access tokens short-lived (≈15 min).
Frontend role checks are UX only — enforce authz on the server.
Route guards prevent navigation, not API access.

topics.title: Environment Variables & Configuration
topics.summary: VITE_/REACT_APP_ prefix for client exposure. Never commit secrets. Use .env files.
topics.explanation: Environment variables control app behavior across environments (dev/staging/prod). In Create React App: only variables prefixed REACT_APP_ are bundled into client JS (everything else is ignored). In Vite: only VITE_ prefixed variables are exposed. NEVER put secrets (API keys, DB passwords) in REACT_APP_/VITE_ variables — they're embedded in the JS bundle and visible to anyone. Use .env.local for local overrides (gitignored), .env.production for prod values (or CI/CD secrets).
topics.analogy: Client env vars are printed on the outside of the package (the JS bundle) — anyone can read them. So only put non-secrets there (a URL is fine), and keep real secrets server-side.
keyPoints: Only VITE_/REACT_APP_/NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars reach the client bundle.
Anything in the client bundle is PUBLIC — never put secrets there.
Secrets live in server env / CI-CD secret stores.
.env.local is gitignored for local dev overrides.
If a secret is committed, rotate it immediately and purge history.

situations.question: Someone committed a secret API key in REACT_APP_STRIPE_SECRET to your repo. What do you do?
situations.answer: Treat it as fully compromised: 1) IMMEDIATELY rotate/revoke the secret in Stripe dashboard — the old key is no longer safe. 2) Remove from code + git history: 'git filter-branch' or BFG Repo Cleaner to purge all history. Force-push. But assume GitHub already cached it if public. 3) Add .env.local to .gitignore if not already. 4) Audit: check if anyone else used the key (Stripe logs show all API calls with the key). 5) Add pre-commit hook: 'git-secrets' or 'detect-secrets' scans for keys before commit. 6) Move to CI/CD secrets (GitHub Actions secrets, Vercel env vars) — never in codebase.

topics.title: React Scheduler & Priority Lanes
topics.summary: React's internal scheduler assigns priority lanes — user input > animations > data updates.
topics.explanation: React's Concurrent Mode uses a priority-based scheduler. Each update gets a 'lane' — a bitmask representing its priority: SyncLane (highest, user input), InputContinuousLane (drag, scroll), DefaultLane (setState), TransitionLane (startTransition), IdleLane (lowest). The scheduler yields to higher-priority work between renders using requestAnimationFrame and postMessage-based task scheduling. This is what makes React Concurrent mode feel instant: user typing always preempts data fetching re-renders.
topics.analogy: React's scheduler is an ER triage nurse: a typing keystroke is a critical patient seen instantly, while a background data re-render waits and can be bumped when something more urgent arrives.
keyPoints: Each update gets a priority 'lane' (Sync, InputContinuous, Default, Transition, Idle).
Higher-priority work preempts lower-priority renders.
User input (SyncLane) beats data updates and transitions.
The scheduler yields between work units to stay responsive.
useTransition/useDeferredValue/Suspense all build on lanes.

situations.question: You wrap a state update in startTransition but the UI still stutters. What are you likely doing wrong?
situations.answer: Common mistakes: 1) The expensive computation happens BEFORE startTransition: const filtered = filterHuge(items, q); startTransition(() => setResults(filtered)); — the computation already blocked the thread. Fix: move the computation INSIDE startTransition. 2) Using useEffect inside the transition — effects are synchronous in commit phase. Move to synchronous computation inside the transition callback. 3) The component tree is deeply un-memoized — transition saves the main thread from blocking but doesn't skip rendering. Add React.memo to children. 4) The 'urgent' state (input value) and 'deferred' state update are coupled. Separate them: setQuery(q) is urgent, setResults inside startTransition is deferred.

situations.question: Explain the difference between Suspense for code splitting vs Suspense for data fetching. Why did only data fetching Suspense come later?
situations.answer: Suspense for code splitting (React 16.6): React.lazy() throws a Promise when the module isn't loaded yet — Suspense catches it, shows fallback, re-renders when Promise resolves. Simple, no state management implications. Suspense for data fetching (React 18+): requires Concurrent Mode because data fetching Suspense is stateful — React must remember where it was in the render tree when it suspended, then resume. Without Concurrent Mode, a suspended render would be lost. The implementation requires: 1) A 'cache' (like React Query cache or React Cache) that stores promises with read() that throws while pending. 2) React's Fiber tree tracks suspended nodes. 3) After hydration (Promise resolves), React replays from the suspended boundary. This is why Suspense data fetching only works with libraries that implement the 'Suspense contract' (TanStack Query, SWR with suspense: true).

topics.title: React Compiler (React Forget)
topics.summary: Auto-memoization compiler — eliminates manual useMemo/useCallback.
topics.explanation: React Compiler (previously 'React Forget') is a build-time compiler that automatically adds memoization to React components. It analyzes your component's code statically, determines which values can be memoized, and inserts the equivalent of useMemo/useCallback/React.memo automatically. This means you write plain JavaScript and the compiler optimizes re-renders. Available in React 19, enabled with the Babel plugin. Caveat: components must follow the Rules of Hooks and must be pure (no side effects in render). Components that break these rules won't be optimized.
topics.analogy: React Compiler is an autopilot for memoization: instead of hand-placing useMemo/useCallback everywhere, you write plain components and the compiler inserts the optimal memoization at build time.
keyPoints: Build-time compiler that auto-memoizes components.
Removes most manual useMemo/useCallback/React.memo.
Requires pure components that follow the Rules of Hooks.
Available with React 19 via a Babel plugin.
Migrate gradually; verify with the Profiler before deleting manual memo.

situations.question: With React Compiler available, should you remove all your existing useMemo and useCallback calls? What's your migration strategy?
situations.answer: Gradual approach: 1) Enable React Compiler with the 'opt-in' setting first — it skips components it can't safely optimize. 2) Use the React Compiler playground to verify what the compiler produces for your components. 3) The compiler can co-exist with manual memoization — it won't double-memoize correctly. 4) Remove useMemo/useCallback only after verifying the compiler optimizes that component (check DevTools Profiler). 5) Keep manual memoization for: external library callbacks that need stable references for imperative APIs, components with mutation-based side effects the compiler can't track. 6) Add 'use no memo' directive on components that intentionally shouldn't be memoized (rare). Net result: less boilerplate, but still understand memoization for cases where compiler can't help.

topics.title: Compound Components & Render Props
topics.summary: Flexible, inversion-of-control component APIs — Radix UI, Headless UI patterns.
topics.explanation: Compound Components expose multiple related sub-components that work together via shared implicit state (Context). The parent manages state, children access it via useContext. This gives consumers total control over layout and composition while the library manages behavior. Used by Radix UI, Headless UI, React Select. Render Props (deprecated by hooks but still seen) pass render functions as props for inversion of control. The key design principle: separate behavior from presentation.
topics.analogy: Compound Components are like LEGO sets: the box (parent) holds the shared studs (Context/state) and you snap together pieces (<Select.Option>) however you like — you control layout, the kit controls behavior.
keyPoints: Related sub-components share implicit state via Context.
Parent manages behavior; consumer controls layout/markup.
Powers Radix UI, Headless UI, React Select.
Core principle: separate behavior from presentation (headless).
Radix's asChild prop lets consumers supply their own element.

situations.question: You're building a design system's Accordion component. How do you design the API to allow custom styling without exposing internal complexity?
situations.answer: Use Compound Components with Headless pattern: 1) AccordionRoot manages open/close state. 2) AccordionItem registers itself, gets an ID. 3) AccordionTrigger reads its item's state from context, applies aria-expanded. 4) AccordionContent conditionally renders. Consumers control all styling, you control all behavior: <Accordion.Root value={open} onValueChange={setOpen}> <Accordion.Item value='item-1'> <Accordion.Trigger className={myStyles.trigger}>Title</Accordion.Trigger> <Accordion.Content>Body</Accordion.Content> </Accordion.Item> </Accordion.Root>. For primitives: expose 'asChild' prop (Radix UI pattern) — renders consumer's element instead of yours: <Accordion.Trigger asChild><button className='my-button'>Styled Trigger</button></Accordion.Trigger>. This achieves 100% styling flexibility with 0% behavior coupling.

topics.title: Micro-Frontends
topics.summary: Module Federation, iframes, Web Components — team independence at scale.
topics.explanation: Micro-frontends extend microservice thinking to the frontend: different teams own different UI sections independently. Approaches: 1) Module Federation (Webpack 5 / Rspack): remote bundles loaded at runtime, share React instances to avoid duplicate React. 2) iframes: true isolation, poor DX, complex communication. 3) Web Components: framework-agnostic, encapsulated. 4) Single-SPA: orchestrator that mounts/unmounts apps. Senior engineers understand the tradeoffs: MFEs solve team scaling but add: network latency (multiple bundles), version coordination, shared state complexity, increased testing surface.
topics.analogy: Micro-frontends are a food court: each stall (team) runs independently with its own kitchen, but they share the seating and entrance (shell). Great for many teams, but you pay for the extra coordination.
keyPoints: Independent teams own independent UI sections, deployed separately.
Module Federation loads remote bundles at runtime.
Share React as a singleton to avoid duplicate copies.
Costs: version coordination, shared state, latency, testing surface.
For small teams, a modular monorepo is usually simpler.

situations.question: Your company wants to split a monolithic React app into micro-frontends managed by 3 different teams. What architecture do you recommend and what are the risks?
situations.answer: Recommendation: Module Federation with a host shell app. Architecture: 1) Shell app: routing, authentication, global state (user, theme), navigation shell. 2) Remote MFEs: isolated domains (catalog, checkout, account). 3) Shared packages: design system, auth library — published as npm packages, not federated. Risks and mitigations: 1) Version drift: teams update React independently — mandate singleton React in shared config and add CI checks. 2) Styling conflicts: use CSS Modules or CSS-in-JS with scoped names. 3) Auth token sharing: shared auth library via postMessage or shared cookie domain. 4) Performance: each MFE is another network request — preload remotes, use HTTP/2 push. 5) Deployment complexity: each MFE deploys independently, breaking changes need coordination — implement API versioning for MFE contracts. For most teams <50 engineers: a well-modularized monorepo is simpler.

topics.title: Advanced Custom Hooks Design
topics.summary: Hooks as abstractions — reusable stateful logic with clean APIs and proper cleanup.
topics.explanation: Expert-level custom hooks encapsulate complex stateful logic with clean external APIs. Key principles: single responsibility, proper cleanup, TypeScript generics for reusability, controlled/uncontrolled variants, and suspense compatibility. Patterns: useControllable (manages state that can be either controlled by parent or internal), useEventCallback (stable callback reference that always calls latest version), useIsomorphicLayoutEffect (SSR-safe), usePrevious (track previous value).
topics.analogy: Advanced custom hooks are like well-designed kitchen appliances: they hide complex machinery (state, cleanup, race handling) behind one or two simple buttons (a clean API).
keyPoints: Encapsulate complex stateful logic behind a clean API.
Principles: single responsibility, proper cleanup, TS generics.
useEventCallback: stable reference that always calls the latest fn.
useControllable: supports controlled and uncontrolled usage.
A real useFetch is basically TanStack Query — prefer the library.

situations.question: You need to build a useFetch hook that handles caching, deduplication, race conditions, and retry. Walk through your implementation.
situations.answer: Layer by layer: 1) State: const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(fetchReducer, { status: 'idle', data: null, error: null }) with clear status types. 2) Abort: const controllerRef = useRef(null); abort on re-fetch. 3) Cache: external Map keyed by URL + params (outside component to survive remounts). Store {data, timestamp}. 4) Deduplication: pending Map keyed by URL — if there's already a pending Promise for this URL, subscribe to it instead of creating a new fetch. 5) Retry: withRetry wrapper around fetch with exponential backoff. 6) Cleanup: return () => controllerRef.current?.abort() from useEffect. Warning: this is essentially TanStack Query. In production: use TanStack Query. Build this only as an exercise or if you have very specific constraints TanStack Query doesn't support.

topics.title: SSR Internals & Hydration
topics.summary: renderToString vs renderToPipeableStream. Hydration mismatches. Selective hydration.
topics.explanation: Classic SSR (renderToString): renders the entire React tree to a string on the server, sends it to the client, then React 'hydrates' — attaches event handlers by reconciling the HTML with a re-render. Blocking — the browser waits for the full string. Streaming SSR (renderToPipeableStream, React 18): sends HTML chunks as they're ready, enabling progressive rendering. The browser starts parsing and rendering before the full response arrives. Selective hydration: Suspense boundaries can defer hydration — user-visible parts hydrate first, offscreen parts hydrate later.
topics.analogy: Streaming SSR is serving a meal course by course instead of waiting for everything at once: the browser shows the ready parts immediately while slower sections stream in behind a Suspense placeholder.
keyPoints: renderToString blocks until the whole tree renders.
renderToPipeableStream streams HTML chunks progressively.
Hydration attaches event handlers to server HTML.
Suspense enables selective/progressive hydration.
Hydration mismatches come from non-deterministic render (dates, random, window).

situations.question: Your SSR app has hydration mismatches causing 'Text content did not match' errors. How do you diagnose and fix this?
situations.answer: Hydration mismatch: the HTML server sent doesn't match what React renders on client. Common causes: 1) Date/time: server renders '2026-01-01', client re-renders 'today' from new Date() — fix: pass timestamp as prop from server. 2) Random IDs: Math.random() differs between server and client — fix: use useId() which is stable. 3) Window/localStorage access: only exists on client — fix: guard with typeof window !== 'undefined' or useEffect. 4) Browser extensions injecting DOM nodes. 5) Server/client timezone mismatch. Debug: React 18 provides detailed mismatch messages in dev mode showing exact attribute differences. Fix pattern: suppressHydrationWarning={true} on elements where mismatch is intentional (e.g., dynamically styled elements). For third-party widgets: mount them inside a useEffect with createPortal to skip SSR entirely.

situations.question: What is the 'Island Architecture' and how does it differ from full SSR and full CSR?
situations.answer: Island Architecture: the server renders a mostly static HTML page. 'Islands' of interactivity are separately hydrated on the client. Each island is an isolated React tree that hydrates independently. Used by: Astro, Fresh (Deno). Compared to: Full SSR (Next.js pages): entire page is a React tree that hydrates — loads all JS even for static content. Full CSR (Vite SPA): all JS loaded, all rendering on client. Islands: only the JS for interactive islands ships to client. Static content has zero JS. Benefits: minimal JS payload, fast TTI for content-heavy sites, each island hydrates with its own priority. Tradeoffs: islands can't share React state directly (must use external store like nanostores or URL state). Best for: documentation sites, blogs, marketing pages with a few interactive components.

topics.title: ISR & On-Demand Revalidation
topics.summary: Stale-while-revalidate at the infrastructure level — Next.js ISR mechanics.
topics.explanation: ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) combines static generation's speed with SSR's freshness. Pages are pre-built statically but revalidated at a configurable interval (revalidate seconds). The first request after expiry triggers background regeneration while serving the stale page (stale-while-revalidate CDN pattern). On-demand ISR: trigger revalidation via a webhook (revalidatePath, revalidateTag) when data actually changes. React 19 extends this via fetch() cache: 'force-cache' with next.tags for granular tag-based invalidation.
topics.analogy: ISR is a bakery that sells the loaf already on the shelf (fast/static) but quietly bakes a fresh one in the background once it's gone stale — so customers get speed and reasonable freshness.
keyPoints: Combines static speed with periodic background freshness.
revalidate seconds = how long a page stays cached before regenerating.
First request after expiry serves stale while regenerating.
On-demand: revalidatePath/revalidateTag via a webhook when data changes.
Tag-based invalidation gives granular cache control.

situations.question: Your e-commerce product pages need to be fast (static) but inventory updates need to reflect within 30 seconds. How do you architect this?
situations.answer: Hybrid approach: 1) Product details (images, description, price) — ISR with revalidate: 3600. Prices rarely change, static is fast. 2) Inventory/stock count — don't statically render. Show a Client Component that fetches stock via a lightweight API route on the client. This way, the static page loads fast, then the stock count updates via client fetch. 3) OR: use on-demand ISR with a webhook from your inventory system. When stock changes, call revalidateTag('product-${id}'). Next request gets fresh data. 4) Stock = 0 edge case: trigger an immediate revalidation via API when stock hits 0 so 'out of stock' shows quickly. Use SWR with refreshInterval: 30000 on the client as a fallback polling mechanism if webhooks fail.

topics.title: ARIA Patterns & WAI-ARIA Spec
topics.summary: Roles, states, properties — ARIA Live Regions, focus management, keyboard navigation.
topics.explanation: Senior engineers implement ARIA beyond basic alt text. Key patterns: 1) Composite widgets (combobox, tree, grid) require roving tabindex for keyboard navigation. 2) ARIA Live Regions (aria-live, aria-atomic, aria-relevant) announce dynamic content to screen readers. 3) Focus management: trap focus in modals (focus-trap-react), return focus on close. 4) Keyboard patterns: arrow keys navigate within widgets, Escape closes overlays, Enter/Space activate. WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide is the specification for these patterns.
topics.analogy: ARIA is the labeling and wiring that lets a screen reader 'see' your custom widgets: roles say what something is, states say what it's doing, and focus management guides the keyboard user through it.
keyPoints: Composite widgets need roving tabindex / aria-activedescendant for keyboard nav.
ARIA live regions announce dynamic content to screen readers.
Trap focus in modals; return focus to the trigger on close.
Keyboard: arrows navigate, Escape closes, Enter/Space activate.
Follow the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide; test with real screen readers.

situations.question: A screen reader user reports that your dropdown menu is completely inaccessible. How do you implement it correctly?
situations.answer: Implement the ARIA Combobox or Menu Button pattern from WAI-ARIA APG: 1) Trigger button: role='button' with aria-expanded and aria-controls pointing to the menu ID. 2) Menu: role='menu' with id matching controls. 3) Menu items: role='menuitem'. 4) Keyboard: Enter/Space opens menu, focuses first item. Arrow keys navigate items (roving tabindex or aria-activedescendant). Escape closes and returns focus to trigger. Home/End jump to first/last. 5) Close on outside click + Escape. 6) For search dropdowns (combobox): role='combobox' on input, role='listbox' on dropdown, role='option' on items, aria-activedescendant tracks focused option. Use Radix UI's DropdownMenu or Headless UI's Menu — they implement these patterns correctly. Testing: test with NVDA (Windows), VoiceOver (Mac), screen reader + keyboard only.

topics.title: Testing Patterns & Anti-patterns
topics.summary: Test behavior not implementation. MSW for network. Playwright for E2E.
topics.explanation: Expert React testing follows 'test behavior, not implementation': query by role/text (not testId), test what users see and do (not state values), use MSW for network layer. Anti-patterns: testing implementation details (useState values, component class names), testing library components you didn't write, over-mocking (mocking fetch module instead of using MSW). Senior engineers also set up Playwright for critical user journeys and use Testing Library's accessibility-first queries.
topics.analogy: Good React tests act like a real user, not an X-ray: they click and read the screen (by role/text) rather than poking at internal state — so they keep passing through refactors that don't change behavior.
keyPoints: Test behavior, not implementation details.
Query by role/text; avoid testId and internal state.
Mock the network at the boundary with MSW, not the fetch module.
Avoid over-mocking and testing third-party components.
Add Playwright for a few critical end-to-end journeys.

situations.question: Your test suite takes 8 minutes to run and CI is slow. How do you cut test time without removing tests?
situations.answer: Systematic approach: 1) Parallelize: Jest --runInBand is serial — use workers (default). Set maxWorkers to 50% of CPUs in CI. 2) Shard: split tests across CI machines (GitHub Actions matrix): jest --shard=1/4, 2/4, 3/4, 4/4. 3) Change detection: in CI, run only tests related to changed files: jest --onlyChanged or Nx affected:test. 4) Fix slow tests: use jest.useFakeTimers() for tests with real timeouts (setTimeout), mock heavy external modules. 5) Separate unit from E2E: run units on every push, E2E on PR merge only. 6) Use Vitest instead of Jest — native ESM, much faster HMR and test discovery. 7) Check for test isolation issues: tests that fail in parallel but not serial — shared global state between tests.

topics.title: Playwright E2E Testing
topics.summary: Cross-browser E2E. Parallel execution. Component testing. Network interception.
topics.explanation: Playwright is the modern E2E testing framework. Advantages over Cypress: multi-browser (Chrome, Firefox, WebKit), multiple tabs/windows, native mobile emulation, better parallel execution, and network interception that works across all browsers. For React: Playwright Component Testing allows rendering components in a real browser without a full app server. Key patterns: locators (get by role, text, label for accessibility-aware querying), network mocking, visual regression testing, auth state sharing.
topics.analogy: Playwright is a robot that drives real browsers like a user — clicking, typing, navigating across Chrome/Firefox/WebKit — to verify the whole app actually works end to end.
keyPoints: Modern cross-browser E2E (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) with mobile emulation.
Accessibility-aware locators (getByRole/Label/Text).
Built-in network interception and parallel execution.
Share auth via storageState so tests don't re-login.
Use trace/screenshot on failure for fast debugging.

situations.question: How do you handle authentication in Playwright tests so every test doesn't have to log in from scratch?
situations.answer: Use Playwright's storageState for session sharing: 1) Create a global setup file that logs in once: authenticate with credentials, save context storageState to a file (auth.json). 2) Configure playwright.config.ts to use this storageState for all tests. 3) Tests start already authenticated. For multiple user roles: create separate storageState files (admin.json, editor.json). Use project dependencies: { name: 'as-admin', use: { storageState: 'admin.json' } }. For tests that require a fresh login (testing login page itself): override with { storageState: { cookies: [], origins: [] } } to clear auth. Advanced: use API to create test users programmatically rather than UI-based login — faster and more reliable.
